headline: FDA Sets Meeting on Dietary Supplement Ingredient Scope
featuredAlt: Conference-room table with blank supplement dossiers, ingredient specification folders, and a U.S. regulatory agenda board
description: FDA has scheduled a public meeting on dietary supplement innovation and the scope of dietary ingredients, keeping U.S. compliance strategy and document readiness in focus for ingredient suppliers and brand teams.

sections.heading: What FDA Announced
paragraphs: FDA said on July 31, 2025 that it would hold a public meeting on dietary supplement innovation and the scope of dietary ingredients, with the event scheduled for September 25, 2025.

The agency framed the meeting around how dietary ingredients fit within the current U.S. supplement framework, a topic that directly affects how new or less familiar inputs are evaluated before launch.

sections.heading: Why Scope Matters
paragraphs: For PS, lecithin, and other phospholipid-adjacent materials, scope questions can shape whether a product concept stays within a supplement pathway or requires a different regulatory strategy.
That makes precise ingredient naming, manufacturing descriptions, and prior-market-status records more important than broad marketing language.

sections.heading: Buyer Takeaway
paragraphs: U.S.-focused buyers should treat FDA's meeting as a reminder to keep source identity, manufacturing flow, intended use, and supporting document files organized before a formulation moves into commercialization.
Suppliers that can explain those basics clearly are likely to create fewer delays when customer regulatory teams start asking scope questions.

sources.title: FDA Announces Public Meeting on Dietary Supplement Innovation and the Scope of Dietary Ingredients

headline: NIQ Flags Fast Korean Growth for Phosphatidylserine Products
featuredAlt: Korean e-commerce analytics desk with abstract sales charts, capsules, and a shopper trend dashboard
description: NIQ says South Korea's online cognitive-enhancement segment for Gen X posted sharp growth, with phosphatidylserine among the fastest-rising ingredients in the category.

sections.heading: What NIQ Reported
paragraphs: In an October 2, 2025 Business Wire release, NIQ said Gen X spending in APAC is rising and highlighted South Korea as a standout market for online cognitive-enhancement products.
The company said that South Korea's online cognitive-enhancement segment grew 168 percent, and it specifically cited phosphatidylserine among the ingredients gaining traction.

sections.heading: Why Buyers Should Watch It
paragraphs: Ingredient demand signals matter even when they come from one channel or one geography because they can influence sourcing plans, safety-stock assumptions, and how suppliers prioritize assays and pack sizes.
For PS suppliers, Korean growth is also a reminder that demand is no longer limited to classic memory positioning in Western markets.

sections.heading: Buyer Takeaway
paragraphs: Teams planning for East Asia should check whether rising retail demand is supported by the right local document set, source preference, and formulation format rather than treating volume growth alone as a supply green light.
A sharper demand forecast only helps if it is matched with realistic lead-time, specification, and registration planning.

sources.title: NIQ: Gen X in APAC to Spend USD 4.4 Trillion by End of 2025, Rising to USD 5.7 Trillion by 2030

headline: Golden Key Launches Sunflower-Derived PSgold Ingredient
featuredAlt: Ingredient development bench with sunflower phosphatidylserine powder, tablet tooling, and blend samples for capsule and drink mix formats
description: Golden Key Ingredients has introduced PSgold, a powdered sunflower-derived phosphatidylserine positioned for capsule, tablet, and powdered-mix formulations.

sections.heading: What Launched
paragraphs: Golden Key Ingredients said on July 1, 2025 that it was introducing PSgold, a sunflower-derived phosphatidylserine in powdered form.
The company positioned the ingredient for capsule, tablet, and powdered-mix applications, making the launch directly relevant to formulation teams that need a non-soy PS option.

sections.heading: Why It Matters
paragraphs: Supplier-side launches matter because they can expand source options in a category where buyers often balance allergen positioning, cost-in-use, and finished-format behavior at the same time.
A sunflower-derived powdered PS also keeps attention on flowability, carrier choice, oxidation control, and spec consistency rather than source language alone.

sections.heading: Buyer Takeaway
paragraphs: Buyers comparing new PS offers should request assay method, carrier disclosure, particle behavior, and the usual quality pack before treating a launch announcement as a qualified supply option.
The strongest commercial fit will depend on how well the powder performs in the intended delivery system, not just on a sunflower-origin claim.

sources.title: Golden Key Ingredients Introduces PSgold PhosphatidylSerine

headline: O Positiv Adds PS to New MENO Brain Retail Launch
featuredAlt: Midlife wellness retail display with unbranded memory-support capsules, planner pages, and a mass-market shelf concept
description: O Positiv says its new MENO Brain product uses phosphatidylserine in a menopause-focused brain-fog concept, adding another mass-retail demand signal for cognitive-positioned ingredients.

sections.heading: What Entered Retail
paragraphs: O Positiv said on January 13, 2026 that it was expanding at Target with three new women's health innovations, including MENO Brain.
The release positioned MENO Brain around brain fog, memory, and cognition, and listed phosphatidylserine among the ingredients in the formula.

sections.heading: What It Signals
paragraphs: This is notable because it places PS inside a menopause and midlife-wellness retail concept rather than a traditional healthy-aging or student-focus story.
That kind of repositioning can change the way buyers think about price targets, co-ingredient compatibility, and the documentation needed for broader consumer-audience scrutiny.

sections.heading: Buyer Takeaway
paragraphs: Ingredient teams tracking women's health should watch where PS appears in stress, hormone, or cognition-adjacent concepts and whether that shifts preferred source, format, or technical substantiation.
Retail expansion is a demand signal, not a claim shortcut, so compliant market translation still matters.

sources.title: O Positiv Expands at Target with Three New Women's Health Innovations

headline: Life Time Uses PS in Melatonin-Free Sleep Formula
featuredAlt: Nightstand wellness scene with unbranded sleep-support capsules, magnesium-style powder scoop, and soft low-light bedroom setting
description: Life Time's new LTH Dream formula includes phosphatidylserine alongside magnesium, pharmaGABA, and other sleep-support ingredients in a melatonin-free format.

sections.heading: What Launched
paragraphs: Life Time announced on August 12, 2025 that it was launching LTH Dream, a melatonin-free sleep formula backed by clinical research messaging.
The release listed phosphatidylserine with magnesium glycinate, pharmaGABA, and other ingredients, showing PS in another stress-and-sleep adjacent blend rather than a standalone cognition concept.

sections.heading: Formulation Signal
paragraphs: PS appearing in sleep products matters to ingredient buyers because it broadens the range of applications where phospholipid ingredients must work inside multi-active systems.
That raises more practical questions on taste, stability, moisture control, and how the ingredient behaves alongside calming actives in different delivery formats.

sections.heading: Buyer Takeaway
paragraphs: Suppliers serving this type of concept should expect more requests around compatibility, handling, and claim discipline than around source identity alone.
A launch like this is best read as a formulation-direction signal for cognitive-nutrition ingredients, not as automatic evidence for every market's sleep claims.

sources.title: Life Time Disrupts Sleep Supplement Market with Launch of LTH Dream: Melatonin-Free Formula Backed by Clinical Research

headline: Sunflower PS Trial in Healthy Children Finds Mixed Results
featuredAlt: Child cognition study setup with memory cards, sunflower phosphatidylserine sachets, and a quiet classroom testing desk
description: A 2025 randomized trial of sunflower phosphatidylserine in healthy children found no significant overall differences on primary or secondary outcomes, while suggesting lower-performing subgroups may warrant further study.

sections.heading: What Was Studied
paragraphs: The Nutritional Journal paper evaluated sunflower phosphatidylserine supplementation in healthy children aged 8 to 12 years using a randomized controlled design.
That makes it relevant for buyers watching whether PS evidence is moving beyond older-adult cognition and into younger age-group product concepts.

sections.heading: What Researchers Found
paragraphs: According to the PubMed summary, the trial found no differences in the primary and secondary outcomes overall.
The same summary says the authors believe future work should focus on children with below-median performance, who may be more likely to benefit from supplementation.

sections.heading: Buyer Takeaway
paragraphs: For ingredient buyers, this is a reminder that pediatric or family-positioned concepts need sharper population targeting than broad 'healthy children' messaging might imply.
Null or mixed results can still be commercially useful because they narrow where follow-up formulation and substantiation work should be focused.

sources.title: The cognitive effects of supplementation with sunflower phosphatidyl serine in healthy children aged 8 to 12 years: a randomized controlled trial
The cognitive effects of supplementation with sunflower phosphatidyl serine in healthy children aged 8 to 12 years: a randomized controlled trial - PMC

headline: PS Supplement Trial Reports Short-Term Memory Gains in MCI
featuredAlt: Clinical memory-assessment room with phosphatidylserine supplement packs, note cards, and an older adult trial workstation
description: A randomized placebo-controlled trial in Chinese older adults with mild cognitive impairment reported that a phosphatidylserine-containing supplement improved several cognitive measures, especially short-term memory.

sections.heading: What Was Tested
paragraphs: A paper indexed by PubMed in January 2025 examined a food supplement containing phosphatidylserine in Chinese older adults with mild cognitive impairment using a randomized, double-blind, placebo-controlled design.
This is a clinically focused setting, so the study matters more for technical evidence mapping than for direct consumer claim copying.

sections.heading: What the Paper Reported
paragraphs: The PubMed summary says the supplement could improve different cognitive functions in the MCI group, especially short-term memory.
The same summary notes increases in serum n-3 PUFAs and neurotransmitter levels, with serum ALA described as a possible mediator.

sections.heading: Buyer Takeaway
paragraphs: Ingredient buyers should separate a disease-population trial from general-market claim use, but the paper is still a useful signal on dose framing and co-ingredient design in cognition products.
Studies like this also reinforce the value of documenting exactly what was in the tested formula rather than treating 'PS' as a uniform evidence category.

sources.title: Effects of a food supplement containing phosphatidylserine on cognitive function in Chinese older adults with mild cognitive impairment: A randomized double-blind, placebo-controlled trial

headline: Choline Form Study Shows Genotype-Linked Response Differences
featuredAlt: Metabolomics lab scene with blank sample vials, molecular pathway notes, and choline ingredient powders under cool lighting
description: A 2025 Frontiers in Nutrition study found that different supplemental choline forms produced distinct one-carbon metabolite patterns, with genetic variation influencing the response.

sections.heading: What Researchers Studied
paragraphs: The Frontiers in Nutrition paper examined healthy adult males receiving different forms of choline and tracked the resulting one-carbon metabolite responses.
The study sits at the intersection of ingredient form selection and precision-nutrition thinking, which is increasingly relevant for advanced cognitive and healthy-aging product design.

sections.heading: What the Study Found
paragraphs: According to the PubMed summary, unique metabolite signatures emerged in response to different supplemental forms of choline.
The same summary says genetic variations that regulate choline homeostasis helped drive those response differences, highlighting nutrient-gene interactions rather than a one-form-fits-all outcome.

sections.heading: Buyer Takeaway
paragraphs: For buyers, the practical point is that form selection may become a more important strategic choice as personalized nutrition and biomarker-aware product design move forward.
That makes raw-material identity and form-specific substantiation more valuable than broad choline equivalence arguments.

sources.title: Common genetic polymorphisms define one-carbon metabolite responses to different forms of choline in healthy adult males
Common genetic polymorphisms define one-carbon metabolite responses to different forms of choline in healthy adult males - PMC

headline: A-GPC Trial Reports Acute Cognitive Lift in Healthy Men
featuredAlt: Sports-cognition lab with stopwatch, Stroop-style test cards, and alpha-GPC powder samples beside shaker bottles
description: A 2024 Nutrients study found that alpha-glycerylphosphorylcholine supplementation improved Stroop-test performance in healthy young men, reinforcing demand for fast-acting choline formats in cognitive products.

sections.heading: What Was Tested
paragraphs: A Nutrients paper indexed on PubMed in December 2024 examined acute alpha-glycerylphosphorylcholine supplementation in healthy men.
A-GPC is a choline-based cognitive ingredient with relevance beyond supplements positioned purely around memory because it also appears in focus and performance-oriented concepts.

sections.heading: What the Study Reported
paragraphs: The PubMed summary says both high-dose and low-dose A-GPC supplementation significantly increased cognitive performance as measured by changes in Stroop total score and Stroop completion time.
That result keeps attention on choline format choice when product teams want a faster-acting cognition story rather than a long-term healthy-aging narrative.

sections.heading: Buyer Takeaway
paragraphs: Buyers following nootropic and active-lifestyle categories should expect continuing demand for ingredients that can be framed around acute-use concepts, but they still need disciplined translation from lab tasks to market claims.
A-GPC's growth also reinforces a broader point for phospholipid-adjacent buyers: form matters, and use-case framing matters just as much.

sources.title: Acute Alpha-Glycerylphosphorylcholine Supplementation Enhances Cognitive Performance in Healthy Men

headline: Choline Review Calls for Better Lifespan Evidence and Precision Tools
featuredAlt: Scientific review desk with lifespan nutrition diagrams, choline pathway sketches, and policy-style research briefing notes
description: A 2025 Perspective on future directions in choline says key questions remain around brain, liver, and cardiometabolic effects, highlighting ongoing demand for stronger biomarkers and more precise nutrition work.

sections.heading: What the Perspective Covers
paragraphs: The 2025 PubMed-indexed Perspective 'Future Directions in Choline' reviews how an essential nutrient identified decades ago still has unresolved questions across neurodevelopment, liver biology, and cardiometabolic health.
The paper summarizes emerging insights presented at a choline-focused scientific symposium, making it a useful map of where expert attention is shifting next.

sections.heading: Why It Matters
paragraphs: For ingredient buyers, the message is that evidence expectations are moving beyond simple presence-of-choline arguments toward biomarker quality, target population clarity, and form-specific reasoning.
That matters for phosphatidylcholine, lecithin, A-GPC, and other choline-adjacent ingredients that may be sold into very different nutrition stories.

sections.heading: Buyer Takeaway
paragraphs: A stronger technical file will increasingly need to explain why a specific form was chosen for a specific population and use case, not just confirm that choline is included.
Suppliers that can support that discussion with better composition and application logic should be better positioned as scientific scrutiny rises.

sources.title: Future Directions in Choline: From Neurodevelopment to Cardiometabolic Health

headline: FDA Issues No-Questions Letter for Sunflower Lecithin GRAS Notice
featuredAlt: Regulatory affairs desk with sunflower lecithin samples, sunflower seeds, and approval-style ingredient documents
description: FDA's GRAS inventory now shows a no-questions response for a sunflower lecithin notice, adding a current U.S. reference point for buyers tracking source options and technical dossiers.

sections.heading: What FDA Posted
paragraphs: FDA's GRAS notice inventory lists GRN 001267 for sunflower lecithin with a February 17, 2026 status stating that the agency has no questions at this time regarding the notifier's conclusion that the ingredient is generally recognized as safe under the intended conditions of use.
The posting gives buyers a current public reference for a sunflower-derived lecithin submission rather than relying only on older market practice or private supplier claims.

sections.heading: Why It Matters
paragraphs: Sunflower-origin materials matter to brands that want a non-soy positioning or need an additional phospholipid source in food and supplement systems.

A no-questions outcome does not remove the need for application-specific due diligence, but it does signal that identity, manufacturing, and use-level arguments were organized well enough to pass FDA's initial review.

sections.heading: Buyer Takeaway
paragraphs: Procurement teams comparing lecithin and phospholipid sources should ask suppliers for the same core file elements visible in GRAS work: source description, process narrative, compositional specifications, and intended-use framing.
That document discipline becomes more important when sunflower-derived inputs are being qualified as a substitute or second source in existing formulations.

sources.title: GRAS Notices
GRAS Notice No. GRN 001267

headline: Bunge Closes IFF Lecithin and Soy Ingredients Deal
featuredAlt: Industrial ingredient logistics scene with lecithin sample jar, soy materials, bulk tanks, and rail-linked shipping infrastructure
description: Bunge says it has closed its acquisition of IFF's soy protein concentrate, lecithin, and crush assets, a move that strengthens scale in protein and phospholipid-adjacent ingredient supply.

sections.heading: What Closed
paragraphs: Bunge announced on March 2, 2026 that it completed the acquisition of International Flavors & Fragrances' soy protein concentrate, lecithin, and soy crush businesses.
The company said the transaction leaves it better positioned to serve customers looking for higher-protein and lecithin ingredient solutions.

sections.heading: Supply-Side Signal
paragraphs: For buyers, the notable point is not just ownership change. The combination of crush, protein, and lecithin assets suggests tighter integration across oilseed processing and downstream specialty ingredient output.
That can affect sourcing leverage, regional availability, and the way suppliers package lecithin supply with other soy-derived ingredients.

sections.heading: Buyer Takeaway
paragraphs: Ingredient teams should watch how portfolio integration changes lead times, specification management, and minimum-order flexibility for lecithin and phospholipid-related materials.
A change in ownership is also a useful trigger to refresh vendor files, approved-site lists, and quality-contact details before the next buying cycle.

sources.title: Bunge Completes Acquisition of IFF’s Soy Protein Concentrate, Lecithin, and Crush Business

headline: Dietary Supplement Listing Bill Returns to U.S. Senate
featuredAlt: Committee hearing room with blank supplement cartons, stacked binders, and a tablet showing abstract compliance charts
description: A CRN-backed U.S. Senate proposal would create a federal dietary supplement registry, keeping market attention on listing, traceability, and product-file readiness.

sections.heading: What Was Reintroduced
paragraphs: The Council for Responsible Nutrition said on January 15, 2026 that the Dietary Supplement Listing Act of 2026 had been reintroduced in the U.S. Senate.
CRN described the proposal as part of a long-running effort to establish a federal dietary supplement registry and modernize marketplace oversight.

sections.heading: Why Documentation Matters
paragraphs: A registry model would increase pressure on brand owners and their suppliers to keep ingredient identity, manufacturer information, and product records organized in a form that can be listed and updated efficiently.
That matters for PS and phospholipid buyers because traceability gaps often show up first in source documentation, specification history, and manufacturer naming consistency.

sections.heading: Buyer Takeaway
paragraphs: Even before any bill becomes law, buyers can use the proposal as a checklist prompt: confirm exact ingredient naming, plant and packer details, and the completeness of each document pack tied to a finished SKU.
Suppliers that can support cleaner listing-style records are likely to create fewer downstream compliance delays.

sources.title: CRN-Backed Product Listing Proposal Reintroduced, Advancing Long-Standing Push for Transparency in Dietary Supplements

headline: AddiVance Launch Highlights PS in Family Focus Formula
featuredAlt: Home study desk with blank routine cards, pencils, water glass, and an unbranded divided tray of capsules and softgels
description: Medella Springs says its new AddiVance formula is positioned for daily focus and attention support, adding another current phosphatidylserine use case in family-oriented supplement marketing.

sections.heading: What Launched
paragraphs: Medella Springs announced AddiVance on June 1, 2026 as a drug-free option positioned for daily focus, attention, and cognitive support.
The release adds another current example of phosphatidylserine appearing in routine cognitive-support formulas rather than only in memory-specific products.

sections.heading: How PS Was Positioned
paragraphs: The company framed the product around families navigating attention and focus concerns, showing that PS continues to be used in consumer concepts that sit between cognition, learning routines, and everyday performance.
That positioning matters for ingredient buyers because it broadens the commercial contexts where PS dosage, taste-masking, and cost-in-use need to work.

sections.heading: Buyer Takeaway
paragraphs: Suppliers serving this type of launch need to support clean source documentation, age-appropriate formulation planning, and a realistic cost structure at consumer serving sizes.
Buyers should also separate ingredient facts from retail messaging and review market-specific claim boundaries before adapting similar concepts.

sources.title: AddiVance Offers Families a Drug-Free Option for Daily Focus, Attention, and Cognitive Support

headline: Midi Health Debuts Women 40+ Supplement Line With PS Formula
featuredAlt: Midlife wellness desk with tea mug, capsule organizer, reading glasses, and neutral supplement packaging silhouettes
description: Midi Health's first supplement line includes a Daily Cortisol Support formula with phosphatidylserine, extending PS use into women 40+ hormone and stress-positioned products.

sections.heading: What Entered the Market
paragraphs: Midi Health announced on August 6, 2025 that it was launching its first supplement line tailored to women 40 and older.
The launch included a Daily Cortisol Support formula that uses phosphatidylserine within a broader women's midlife wellness concept.

sections.heading: Why It Matters
paragraphs: This is a useful market signal because it places PS in a hormone- and stress-adjacent supplement story rather than a classic memory-only pitch.
That kind of crossover positioning can change formulation priorities around flavor systems, serving format, and how PS is combined with botanicals or other actives.

sections.heading: Buyer Takeaway
paragraphs: Ingredient teams following women's health launches should watch where PS is being grouped with stress, aging, or hormonal-support narratives and whether those combinations affect source choice or target assay.
As always, buyers need to separate company marketing language from what their own markets permit on-pack or in technical sales material.

sources.title: Midi Health Launches Its First-Ever Supplement Line Tailored to Women 40+

headline: MFGM Phospholipid Trial Shows Limited Cognitive Effect in Older Adults
featuredAlt: Clinical assessment table with face-down memory cards, sachets of nutrition powder, and an older adult participating in a cognition task
description: A randomized trial of milk fat globule membrane phospholipids in older adults found no significant overall cognitive effect, though the high-dose group showed better subjective alertness during testing.

sections.heading: What Was Studied
paragraphs: Researchers tested milk fat globule membrane phospholipid supplementation in older adults with subjective memory complaints using a randomized, double-blind, placebo-controlled design.
The work examined cognition in a group where phospholipid support is commercially relevant but where evidence in otherwise healthy older adults has remained limited.

sections.heading: What the Trial Reported
paragraphs: According to the PubMed summary, the study did not find significant overall effects on cognitive function.
The same summary says subjective alertness during testing improved in the high-dose group, alongside reductions in inflammation-related and lipid markers discussed by the authors.

sections.heading: Buyer Takeaway
paragraphs: For buyers in healthy-aging categories, the result is a reminder that phospholipid-rich ingredients may generate nuanced outcomes rather than a simple headline efficacy claim.
Study population, endpoint choice, and dose level all matter when deciding how much evidence is strong enough for a formulation story or technical sales file.

sources.title: The effect of milk fat globule membrane phospholipid supplementation on cognitive function in older adults with subjective memory complaints-a randomized, double-blind, placebo-controlled trial
The effect of milk fat globule membrane phospholipid supplementation on cognitive function in older adults with subjective memory complaints-a randomized, double-blind, placebo-controlled trial - PMC

headline: Sunflower Lecithin Dry-Eye Trial Finds No Clear Benefit
featuredAlt: Ophthalmology exam station with diagnostic equipment and a clear dish of sunflower lecithin softgels
description: A recent dry-eye study of sunflower lecithin supplementation reported no significant improvement versus placebo, offering a cautionary evidence point for phosphatidylcholine-rich positioning.

sections.heading: What Was Tested
paragraphs: The published study evaluated sunflower lecithin supplementation in adults with dry eye disease linked to meibomian gland dysfunction.

Because sunflower lecithin is rich in phosphatidylcholine, the trial is relevant to buyers watching how mainstream phospholipid ingredients are being translated into condition-specific supplement concepts.

sections.heading: What the Paper Found
paragraphs: The article reports no significant improvement compared with placebo on the main outcomes assessed in the study.
That makes the paper a useful balancing data point for companies considering more ambitious application stories around lipid-support mechanisms.

sections.heading: Buyer Takeaway
paragraphs: Ingredient buyers should treat mechanism-based narratives and finished-product evidence as separate questions, especially when a common food ingredient is repositioned into a symptom-led supplement category.
Null or limited findings can still be commercially useful because they sharpen expectations on dose, population choice, and claim discipline.

sources.title: Effect of Sunflower Lecithin Supplementation on Meibomian Gland Function in Adults With Dry Eye Disease

headline: Review Maps Choline Gaps Across Pediatric Formula and Supplement Use
featuredAlt: Pediatric nutrition planning setup with powder scoop, plain bottle, blank sachets, and an abstract molecular model
description: A pediatric nutrition review argues that choline supply should be assessed more carefully across formulas, fortifiers, and supplements, keeping attention on phosphatidylcholine-linked ingredient design in early-life products.

sections.heading: What the Review Covered
paragraphs: The PubMed-listed review examines how choline is supplied through pediatric formulas, fortifiers, and supplements across age groups and clinical indications.
It frames choline as important for membrane-related functions through compounds such as phosphatidylcholine and sphingomyelin, as well as for one-carbon metabolism.

sections.heading: Why It Matters
paragraphs: For B2B ingredient teams, the article is a reminder that early-life nutrition products are not just about headline nutrient inclusion. Form, delivery route, and compatibility with broader formula systems all matter.
That is especially relevant where phospholipid-rich ingredients or choline donors are being considered for specialized pediatric or medical-nutrition applications.

sections.heading: Buyer Takeaway
paragraphs: Buyers serving pediatric categories should expect tighter questions on nutrient form, intended age range, and how an ingredient fits into complete feeding systems rather than a single active narrative.
A stronger supplier conversation will include composition details, use-case boundaries, and the practical reason a given choline or phospholipid form was selected.

sources.title: Choline in Pediatric Nutrition: Assessing Formula, Fortifiers and Supplements Across Age Groups and Clinical Indications

headline: Meta-Analysis Backs Further Choline Work in Alcohol-Exposed Pregnancies
featuredAlt: Maternal nutrition counseling table with prenatal capsules, blank clinical review sheets, and clinician-patient hands in discussion
description: A 2025 meta-analysis supports further study of choline supplementation during or after alcohol-exposed pregnancies, keeping prenatal choline evidence in focus for specialized nutrition developers.

sections.heading: What Was Analyzed
paragraphs: The Pediatrics and Neonatology meta-analysis reviewed whether choline supplementation during or after pregnancies exposed to alcohol can improve children's neurocognitive development.
The topic sits close to prenatal and early-life nutrition strategy, where choline form and evidence quality matter more than simple nutrient presence.

sections.heading: What the Evidence Suggests
paragraphs: The paper argues that choline remains a credible area for further work in this population, rather than a closed question with settled commercial answers.
That kind of result is important for ingredient developers because it supports continued research interest while still signaling that study design and clinical context need careful handling.

sections.heading: Buyer Takeaway
paragraphs: Suppliers discussing prenatal or pediatric choline ingredients should be prepared to separate general biological plausibility from finished-product evidence in sensitive populations.
Buyers should expect especially high scrutiny on technical documentation, intended-use boundaries, and the way any study is translated into product communication.

sources.title: Does supplementation of choline during or after pregnancies exposed to alcohol improve neurocognitive development of children? A meta-analysis

headline: Lecithin Study Links Cognitive and Muscle Support Signals in Older Adults
featuredAlt: Active aging assessment scene with lecithin granules, a hand-grip tester, and memory-task tools in a clinical setting
description: A 2025 lecithin study in Chinese older adults and SAMP8 mice connects memory and muscle endpoints, widening interest in lecithin beyond standard emulsifier or choline-supply discussions.

sections.heading: What Researchers Reported
paragraphs: The Advanced Science paper combines work in Chinese older adults with parallel animal research in SAMP8 mice.
According to the PubMed summary, the study targets the overlap between sarcopenia and mild cognitive impairment as a possible route for prevention-oriented nutrition research.

sections.heading: Why Ingredient Teams Watch It
paragraphs: Lecithin is often treated as a familiar functional input, but this paper pushes it into a more integrated healthy-aging discussion covering both memory and muscle-related outcomes.
That does not create a direct market claim on its own, but it does broaden the technical conversation around which lecithin-derived components may matter in future formulation work.

sections.heading: Buyer Takeaway
paragraphs: Buyers following healthy-aging ingredients should note where common phospholipid materials are being re-examined through multi-endpoint study designs rather than single-function narratives.
Any commercial use still depends on form, specification detail, and how carefully the evidence is translated into compliant market language.

sources.title: Lecithin Alleviates Memory Deficits and Muscle Attenuation in Chinese Older Adults and SAMP8 Mice

headline: New Wellex Formula Combines Phosphatidylserine, Citicoline and Creatine
featuredAlt: Laboratory technician measuring an ingredient on a precision scale
imageCredit: Photo by Nathan Rimoux on Unsplash
description: Wellex has launched a powder supplement combining phosphatidylserine, citicoline and creatine, reflecting continued use of PS in multi-ingredient nutrition products.

sections.heading: What Was Announced
paragraphs: Kyowa Hakko USA announced on June 9, 2026 that Wellex had launched Creatine Well, a women's supplement formulated with Cognizin citicoline, Pürest Creatine and Sharp-PS phosphatidylserine.

sections.heading: Formula and Format
paragraphs: According to the announcement, the product is an unflavored powder with no sweeteners, flavors, caffeine or fillers. The release positions the three-ingredient formula around cognition, strength, recovery and healthy aging.

sections.heading: Relevance for Ingredient Buyers
paragraphs: The launch is a current example of phosphatidylserine being used in a multi-ingredient powder rather than only in tablets or capsules. Buyers evaluating similar concepts still need to confirm source, assay, carrier, powder behavior, serving size and permitted market claims for the finished product.

sources.title: Kyowa Hakko USA's Cognizin® Citicoline Featured in Wellex's New Creatine Well
